subject: mathematics
title: Three Dimensional Geometry
narrative: This rationalised NCERT chapter covers the geometry of LINES in space (direction cosines/ratios, the vector and Cartesian equations of a line, the angle between lines, skew lines and the shortest distance). Planes are not part of this chapter's source, so no plane nodes are authored. Every node below has a clean verbatim source statement.

nodes.title: Find the direction cosines of a line
nodes.brief: Direction cosines l, m, n are the cosines of the angles a directed line makes with the x-, y-, and z-axes; they satisfy l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1.
nodes.quote: are called direction cosines of the directed line L.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What direction cosines are.
A directed line L through the origin makes angles alpha, beta, gamma with the x-, y-, and z-axes respectively; the cosines cos(alpha), cos(beta), cos(gamma) are called the direction cosines of L, written l, m, n. For example, if L makes 60 degrees with the x-axis, 90 degrees with the y-axis, and 30 degrees with the z-axis, then l = 1/2, m = 0, n = sqrt(3)/2.

PART 2 — The identity l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1.
Because l, m, n are cosines of angles summing geometrically over three orthogonal axes, they always satisfy l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1. This is a unit-vector identity: (l, m, n) is the unit vector along the line. Check: (1/2)^2 + 0^2 + (sqrt(3)/2)^2 = 1/4 + 0 + 3/4 = 1. Good.

PART 3 — Reversing the line flips the signs.
If the line is reversed, each angle changes to its supplement (pi minus the angle), so each cosine changes sign: l, m, n become -l, -m, -n. A given undirected line therefore has two sets of direction cosines (one for each direction); we fix a direction to get a unique set.
keyMoves: Find the angles the directed line makes with each axis
Take the cosines as l, m, n
Note reversing the line flips the signs of l, m, n
misconceptions: Forgetting that reversing the line direction reverses the signs
Ignoring the constraint l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1

nodes.title: Find the direction ratios of a line
nodes.brief: Direction ratios are any three numbers proportional to the direction cosines; normalise by dividing by sqrt(a^2+b^2+c^2) to recover the direction cosines.
nodes.quote: Any three numbers which are proportional to the direction cosines of a line are called the *direction ratios* of the line.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What direction ratios are.
Any three numbers a, b, c proportional to the direction cosines l, m, n are called direction ratios of the line: a = lambda*l, b = lambda*m, c = lambda*n for some non-zero real lambda. For the line with direction cosines 2/3, -1/3, -2/3, one set of direction ratios is 2, -1, -2 (choosing lambda = 3); another equally valid set is -4, 2, 4 (lambda = -6).

PART 2 — Recovering direction cosines from direction ratios.
Given direction ratios a, b, c, the direction cosines are l = a/sqrt(a^2+b^2+c^2), m = b/sqrt(a^2+b^2+c^2), n = c/sqrt(a^2+b^2+c^2) (with consistent sign choice). For a = 2, b = -1, c = -2: sqrt(4+1+4) = 3, so l = 2/3, m = -1/3, n = -2/3.

PART 3 — Direction ratios are not unique.
Any non-zero scalar multiple of (a, b, c) is also a valid set of direction ratios for the same line. A direction vector of the line (from the vector equation r = a + lambda*b) directly supplies direction ratios from its components.
keyMoves: Take any three numbers proportional to l, m, n
Recognise a, b, c = lambda(l, m, n)
Normalise (divide by sqrt(a^2 + b^2 + c^2)) to recover the direction cosines
misconceptions: Treating direction ratios as unique (they are only up to a non-zero multiple)
Confusing direction ratios with direction cosines (which are normalised)

nodes.title: Find direction cosines of a line through two points
nodes.brief: Get direction ratios from coordinate differences, then normalise by the distance PQ to obtain the direction cosines.
nodes.quote: we can determine the direction cosines of a line passing through the given points
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Direction ratios from coordinate differences.
For two points P(x1, y1, z1) and Q(x2, y2, z2), the differences x2-x1, y2-y1, z2-z1 are direction ratios of the line PQ (they are the components of the vector PQ). For P(-2, 4, -5) and Q(1, 2, 3): direction ratios are 1-(-2), 2-4, 3-(-5) = 3, -2, 8.

PART 2 — Compute the distance PQ.
PQ = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2 + (z2-z1)^2). For the example above: PQ = sqrt(9 + 4 + 64) = sqrt(77).

PART 3 — Divide to get direction cosines.
Divide each direction ratio by PQ to normalise: l = (x2-x1)/PQ, m = (y2-y1)/PQ, n = (z2-z1)/PQ. For the example: l = 3/sqrt(77), m = -2/sqrt(77), n = 8/sqrt(77). Check: 9/77 + 4/77 + 64/77 = 77/77 = 1. Correct.
keyMoves: Take direction ratios as x2 - x1, y2 - y1, z2 - z1
Compute the distance PQ = sqrt of the sum of their squares
Divide each difference by PQ to get the direction cosines
misconceptions: Forgetting to divide by the distance (leaving direction ratios, not cosines)
Subtracting the coordinates in inconsistent order

nodes.title: Write the vector equation of a line
nodes.brief: A line through point a parallel to vector b is r = a + lambda*b; every point on the line corresponds to exactly one value of the real parameter lambda.
nodes.quote: the vector equation of the line is given by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Building the equation from the geometry.
Let the line pass through the point with position vector a and be parallel to vector b. Any point P with position vector r lies on the line if and only if r - a is parallel to b, i.e. r - a = lambda*b for some real number lambda. Rearranging gives the vector equation: r = a + lambda*b.

PART 2 — Identifying a and b from a problem.
For the line through (5, 2, -4) parallel to 3i + 2j - 8k: a = 5i + 2j - 4k and b = 3i + 2j - 8k, so r = (5i + 2j - 4k) + lambda*(3i + 2j - 8k). The components of b (here 3, 2, -8) are direction ratios of the line.

PART 3 — Lambda traces the whole line.
As lambda ranges over all real numbers, r traces every point on the line: lambda = 0 gives the point a itself, positive lambda moves in the direction of b, negative lambda moves the other way. There is no restriction on lambda; any value yields a valid point on the line.
keyMoves: Identify a (a point on the line) and b (a direction vector)
Write r = a + lambda b
Let lambda range over the reals to trace the whole line
misconceptions: Confusing the direction vector b with its magnitude |b|
Using a position vector of the wrong point for a

nodes.title: Write the Cartesian equation of a line
nodes.brief: The line through (x1,y1,z1) with direction ratios a,b,c satisfies (x-x1)/a = (y-y1)/b = (z-z1)/c; this is obtained by eliminating lambda from the parametric form.
nodes.quote: This is the Cartesian equation of the line.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write the parametric equations.
Starting from r = a + lambda*b with point (x1, y1, z1) and direction ratios a, b, c, the coordinates of a general point on the line are: x = x1 + lambda*a, y = y1 + lambda*b, z = z1 + lambda*c. For the line through (5, 2, -4) with direction ratios 3, 2, -8: x = 5 + 3*lambda, y = 2 + 2*lambda, z = -4 - 8*lambda.

PART 2 — Eliminate lambda.
From each parametric equation, lambda = (x - x1)/a = (y - y1)/b = (z - z1)/c. Setting all three equal gives the Cartesian (symmetric) form: (x - x1)/a = (y - y1)/b = (z - z1)/c. For the example: (x-5)/3 = (y-2)/2 = (z+4)/(-8).

PART 3 — Use direction cosines when given.
If the direction cosines l, m, n are given instead of direction ratios, substitute them in the denominators: (x - x1)/l = (y - y1)/m = (z - z1)/n. The structure is identical; l, m, n are just normalised direction ratios.
keyMoves: Write the parametric equations x = x1 + lambda a, etc.
Eliminate lambda to get (x - x1)/a = (y - y1)/b = (z - z1)/c
Use direction cosines l, m, n in place of a, b, c if given
misconceptions: Putting the point coordinates in the denominators instead of the direction ratios
Failing to keep all three ratios equal

nodes.title: Find the angle between two lines
nodes.brief: The acute angle between two lines equals cos^{-1}(|a1*a2 + b1*b2 + c1*c2| / (|b1||b2|)) using their direction vectors.
nodes.quote: Now, we find the angle between two lines when their equations are given.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Angle via direction vectors.
The angle between two lines equals the angle between their direction vectors b1 and b2. The formula is cos(theta) = |b1.b2| / (|b1| |b2|), where the absolute value ensures we take the acute angle. For b1 = i + 2j + 2k and b2 = 3i + 2j + 6k: b1.b2 = 3 + 4 + 12 = 19, |b1| = 3, |b2| = 7, so cos(theta) = 19/21.

PART 2 — Using direction ratios in Cartesian form.
When lines are in Cartesian form with direction ratios a1,b1,c1 and a2,b2,c2: cos(theta) = |a1*a2 + b1*b2 + c1*c2| / (sqrt(a1^2+b1^2+c1^2) * sqrt(a2^2+b2^2+c2^2)). For lines with direction ratios 3,5,4 and 1,1,2: numerator = 3+5+8 = 16, denominators sqrt(50) and sqrt(6), so cos(theta) = 16/sqrt(300) = 8*sqrt(3)/15.

PART 3 — Using direction cosines.
If direction cosines l1,m1,n1 and l2,m2,n2 are given, the formula simplifies to cos(theta) = |l1*l2 + m1*m2 + n1*n2| because each set is already normalised (l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1). The result is the same acute angle.
keyMoves: Take the direction vectors (direction ratios) of the two lines
Apply cos(theta) = |a1 a2 + b1 b2 + c1 c2| / (|b1| |b2|)
Take the acute angle via the absolute value
misconceptions: Using the points on the lines instead of the direction vectors
Forgetting the absolute value, giving an obtuse angle

nodes.title: Test two lines for perpendicularity or parallelism
nodes.brief: Two lines are perpendicular if a1*a2 + b1*b2 + c1*c2 = 0, and parallel if their direction ratios are proportional.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The perpendicularity condition.
Two lines with direction ratios a1,b1,c1 and a2,b2,c2 are perpendicular (theta = 90 degrees) exactly when their direction vectors have zero dot product: a1*a2 + b1*b2 + c1*c2 = 0. For example, direction ratios 1, -1, 2 and 2, 3, 1/2: dot product = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0, so the lines are perpendicular.

PART 2 — The parallelism condition.
Two lines are parallel (theta = 0) when their direction ratios are proportional: a1/a2 = b1/b2 = c1/c2. For direction ratios 2, 3, 6 and 4, 6, 12: each ratio is 1/2, so the lines are parallel. Equivalently, the direction vectors b1 and b2 are scalar multiples of each other.

PART 3 — These are special cases of the angle formula.
Perpendicularly sets the dot product to zero (cos 90 = 0); parallelism sets the cross product to zero (sin 0 = 0). Both conditions come directly from the general angle formula. When testing, first form the dot product; if zero, perpendicular; then check proportionality; if proportional, parallel; otherwise neither.
keyMoves: Form a1 a2 + b1 b2 + c1 c2 from the direction ratios
If it equals 0, the lines are perpendicular
If a1/a2 = b1/b2 = c1/c2, the lines are parallel
misconceptions: Swapping the perpendicular (dot product 0) and parallel (proportional) conditions
Testing the points instead of the direction ratios

nodes.title: Recognise skew lines
nodes.brief: Skew lines are non-coplanar lines in space that are neither intersecting nor parallel — a situation that cannot occur in 2D.
nodes.quote: such pair of lines are *non coplanar* and are called *skew lines*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What skew lines are.
In 3D space, two lines can fail to meet and yet not be parallel. Such lines are called skew lines: they are non-coplanar (they do not lie in any single plane). A classic example from NCERT: in a room, the diagonal line across the ceiling (GE) and the diagonal line down a wall (DB) are skew — they never meet and are not parallel.

PART 2 — How skew lines differ from parallel and intersecting lines.
Intersecting lines meet at a point (shortest distance = 0) and lie in a common plane. Parallel lines never meet but do lie in a common plane. Skew lines neither meet nor lie in a common plane. The key difference from 2D is that two non-parallel lines in a plane always meet; in 3D they may be skew.

PART 3 — Identifying skew lines.
To confirm two lines are skew: (i) verify they are not parallel (direction ratios not proportional), and (ii) verify they do not intersect (the system of equations for a common point has no solution). If both conditions hold, the lines are skew and the shortest-distance formula applies.
keyMoves: Check the two lines neither intersect nor are parallel
Confirm they do not lie in a common plane (non-coplanar)
Classify such a pair as skew lines
misconceptions: Calling parallel or intersecting lines skew
Thinking any two non-parallel lines must intersect (true in 2D, not 3D)

nodes.title: Define the shortest distance between two lines
nodes.brief: The shortest distance between two lines is the minimum length of any segment with one endpoint on each line; it is zero for intersecting lines and equals the perpendicular distance for parallel lines.
nodes.quote: By the shortest distance between two lines we mean the join of a point in one line with one point on the other line so that the length of the segment so obtained is the smallest.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The definition.
By the shortest distance between two lines we mean the join of a point on one line with a point on the other line such that the length of the segment is the smallest possible. Among all segments connecting the two lines, we seek the one of minimum length.

PART 2 — The three cases.
For intersecting lines the shortest distance is zero (the lines share a point). For parallel lines the shortest distance is the perpendicular distance from any point on one line to the other line. For skew lines neither of these applies, and the shortest-distance segment is the unique common perpendicular — perpendicular to both lines simultaneously.

PART 3 — Why the common perpendicular is shortest for skew lines.
For skew lines the line of shortest distance is perpendicular to both lines; its direction is therefore b1 x b2 (the cross product of the two direction vectors). Any other segment between the lines would have a component along one of the lines, making it longer. This geometric fact drives the formula used in the next node.
keyMoves: Consider segments joining a point on each line
Take the one of smallest length as the shortest distance
Note it is zero for intersecting lines, perpendicular distance for parallel lines
misconceptions: Measuring the distance between arbitrary chosen points rather than the minimum
Assuming the shortest-distance segment is along either line

nodes.title: Compute the shortest distance between two skew lines
nodes.brief: Use d = |(a2 - a1).(b1 x b2)| / |b1 x b2| to compute the shortest distance between two skew lines r = a1 + lambda*b1 and r = a2 + mu*b2.
nodes.quote: the line of the shortest distance will be perpendicular to both the lines.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Read off a1, a2, b1, b2 from the line equations.
Write each line in vector form r = a + (parameter)*b. The position vectors a1 and a2 are points on each line; b1 and b2 are the direction vectors. For r = i + j + lambda*(2i - j + k) and r = 2i + j - k + mu*(3i - 5j + 2k): a1 = i + j, b1 = 2i - j + k, a2 = 2i + j - k, b2 = 3i - 5j + 2k. So a2 - a1 = i - k.

PART 2 — Compute the cross product b1 x b2.
The common perpendicular direction is b1 x b2. Using the determinant: b1 x b2 = |i j k; 2 -1 1; 3 -5 2| = i((-1)(2) - (1)(-5)) - j((2)(2) - (1)(3)) + k((2)(-5) - (-1)(3)) = i(-2+5) - j(4-3) + k(-10+3) = 3i - j - 7k. So |b1 x b2| = sqrt(9 + 1 + 49) = sqrt(59).

PART 3 — Apply the shortest-distance formula.
d = |(a2 - a1).(b1 x b2)| / |b1 x b2|. With a2 - a1 = i - k and b1 x b2 = 3i - j - 7k: dot product = (1)(3) + (0)(-1) + (-1)(-7) = 3 + 0 + 7 = 10. So d = |10| / sqrt(59) = 10/sqrt(59).
keyMoves: Write both lines as a + (parameter) b and read off a1, a2, b1, b2
Compute the common perpendicular direction b1 x b2
Apply d = |(a2 - a1).(b1 x b2)| / |b1 x b2|
misconceptions: Using the formula for parallel lines on skew lines (or vice versa)
Forgetting to take the absolute value / dividing by the wrong magnitude
